texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium is regarded as a valuable metal in the industry with broad applications including lithium-ion batteries, electric vehicles and portable devices [1-3]. The global reserves of this element is finite. Furthermore, due to the drastic increase in demand for lithium in recent years, the supply security for this metal has become a significant issue for many industrial-based countries [4]. Hence, an alternative supply solution for lithium is highly warranted.
texts.text: Lithium is regarded as a valuable metal in the industry with broad applications including lithium-ion batteries, electric vehicles and portable devices [1-3]. The global reserves of this element is finite. Furthermore, due to the drastic increase in demand for lithium in recent years, the supply security for this metal has become a significant issue for many industrial-based countries [4]. Hence, an alternative supply solution for lithium is highly warranted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A pragmatic approach to supplying lithium may be its recovery from salt lake brines. Salt brines are the most abundant lithium sources, comprising approximately 60% of all known lithium deposits [5]. Moreover, producing lithium from salt brine may be more economical than directly extracting it from lithium ores [5]. However, lithium occurs at a low concentration in these brines, and thus, is difficult to recover because of the presence of a much higher concentration of other minerals. Particularly magnesium interferes with the separation process [6].

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work aimed to evaluate the enrichment of lithium (Li) from a simulated salt lake brine by using an integrated nanofiltration (NF) and membrane distillation (MD) process. Two types of NF membranes, namely NF90 and NF270, were employed to compare their performances for Li and magnesium (Mg) rejection under various operating conditions. In the presence of a competing ion ( i.e., Mg) at different concentration, Li rejection by NF90 and NF270 membrane increased, which could be attributed to ion-shielding effects. On the other hand, Li rejection by the NF membranes slightly reduced by increasing the applied pressure from 4 to 8 bar. Increasing the pH from 3 to 11 did not significantly affect Li rejection efficiency. Under optimum operating conditions, the Mg/Li molar ratio changed from 10 to 0.19 after NF90 treatment, and 10 to 2.1 after NF270 treatment. NF90 and NF270 membranes achieved 23 and 44% Li separation, respectively. The separated Li following NF treatments could be further enriched or concentrated significantly (80%) by using the direct contact-MD system. This study demonstrates that an integrated membrane process could be an efficient method for lithium recovery from salt lake brines.

On the other hand, Li rejection by the NF membranes slightly reduced by increasing the applied pressure from 4 to 8 bar. These include solvent extraction [7-12], electrodialysis [13,14], precipitation [15,16] and ion exchange [17,18]. However, all these processes have limitations when the Mg/Li ratio is high [2]. The limitations include the loss of lithium during the extraction process and the requirement of a significant amount of chemical for magnesium reduction before lithium extraction. For example, magnesium has a greater affinity to react with lime as compared to lithium, leading to inefficiency in lime precipitation. Hence, it is essential to develop an innovative process for efficient recovery of lithium from brine sources.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure-driven membrane processes such as nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO) have been investigated for the separation of lithium from salt lake brines [14,19-22]. NF is an attractive option since it usually permeates monovalent ions in preference to multivalent ions [19,23]. For example, Drioli et al. [24] noted that NF could reject 88% of the magnesium content from seawater, while retaining 74% of the lithium. However, the effect of different Mg/Li ratios has not been systematically investigated.

texts.orig: solution for concentrating RO and NF brines [25-28]. MD can concentrate salts up to saturation, allowing downstream crystallization [29]. In addition, solar energy or waste-heat can be utilised, which is useful in remote locations where steam or electricity may not readily be available [30]. As compared to conventional resource recovery methods ( e.g. , solvent extraction, dialysis and precipitation), an integrated membrane separation process may be an effective alternative [31,32]. Different combinations of membrane separation processes ( e.g. , NF-MD and membrane bioreactor-NF/RO) has been assessed for nutrient recovery [33], water reclamation [34,35] and seawater desalination [36]. The performance of the MD process for the regeneration of different liquid desiccant solutions such as lithium chloride has been investigated previously [37,38]. However, an integrated NF-MD process has not been studied for the recovery of metals including lithium. Such an integrated process is necessary when considering the interference of cooccurring salts. For example, during the MD process, dissolved divalent cations such as magnesium can cause membrane scaling that can affect the efficacy of the process [34]. Therefore, an integration of NF with the MD process will reduce the concentration of multi-valent cations that can potentially affect the performance of the MD process during lithium enrichment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, an integrated membrane process using NF and MD for the recovery of lithium from a simulated salt lake brine was investigated. Firstly, the rejection of lithium and magnesium by NF was studied as a function of magnesium concentration, solution pH and operating pressure and membrane type. The NF-permeate was then passed through the MD process to further enrich the lithium content for its recovery.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Feed solution
texts.text: 2.1. Feed solution

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A synthetic wastewater containing the mixture of lithium (Li) and magnesium (Mg) was prepared to simulate the characteristics of a Salt Lake Brine [19]. Analytical grade (purity ≥99.98%) lithium chloride (LiCl2) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2) was used to prepare the simulated feed. Mg/Li ratios of 5, 10, 15 and 20 were used for the experiments. The Mg/Li ratio was varied by increasing the Mg concentration, while Li concentration was kept constant at 141 mg/L. The pH of the feed solution was 6.8.

Analytical grade (purity ≥99.98%) lithium chloride (LiCl2) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2) was used to prepare the simulated feed. Mg/Li ratios of 5, 10, 15 and 20 were used for the experiments. The Mg/Li ratio was varied by increasing the Mg concentration, while Li concentration was kept constant at 141 mg/L. The pH of the feed solution was 6.8.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Lab-scale cross-flow nanofiltration system and operating protocol
texts.text: 2.2. Lab-scale cross-flow nanofiltration system and operating protocol

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, a lab-scale cross-flow filtration system (Figure S1) was used to study the efficacy of nanofiltration (NF) membranes for Li and Mg rejection/separation. The cross-flow filtration system consists of a stainless steel membrane cell that was manufactured in-house. The membrane cell had a channel height of 2 mm, and can house a nanofiltration membrane with an effective surface area of 40 cm 2 (10 cm × 4 cm). In addition, the cross-flow filtration system comprised a high pressure pump (HydraCell pump, Wanner Engineering Inc., Minneapolis, USA); a digital flow meter (Optiflow 1000, Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, USA) attached to a computer for measuring the permeate flux; and a rotameter (PSM312, Techfluid, Singapore) for controlling the cross-flow velocity. During all experiments, the temperature was maintained at room temperature ( i.e., 20 °C) by using a chiller (Neslab RTE 7, Thermo Scientific, Sydney, Australia), while cross-flow velocity was kept constant at 40 cm/s. Two nanofiltration membranes, i.e., NF90 and NF270, with different molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) were purchased from Dow FilmTec (Minneapolis, USA). Both NF90 and NF270 are thin-film composite polyamide membranes, but their MWCO is specified as 200 and 300 g/mol, respectively. Both are specified to have >97% rejection of MgSO4, while NF270 is indicated to pass 40-60% of CaCl2 at 4.8 bar and 25 °C.

The cross-flow filtration system consists of a stainless steel membrane cell that was manufactured in-house. The membrane cell had a channel height of 2 mm, and can house a nanofiltration membrane with an effective surface area of 40 cm 2 (10 cm × 4 cm). In addition, the cross-flow filtration system comprised a high pressure pump (HydraCell pump, Wanner Engineering Inc., Minneapolis, USA); a digital flow meter (Optiflow 1000, Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, USA) attached to a computer for measuring the permeate flux; and a rotameter (PSM312, Techfluid, Singapore) for controlling the cross-flow velocity. During all experiments, the temperature was maintained at room temperature ( i.e., 20 °C) by using a chiller (Neslab RTE 7, Thermo Scientific, Sydney, Australia), while cross-flow velocity was kept constant at 40 cm/s. Two nanofiltration membranes, i.e., NF90 and NF270, with different molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) were purchased from Dow FilmTec (Minneapolis, USA). Both NF90 and NF270 are thin-film composite polyamide membranes, but their MWCO is specified as 200 and 300 g/mol, respectively. Both are specified to have >97% rejection of MgSO4, while NF270 is indicated to pass 40-60% of CaCl2 at 4.8 bar and 25 °C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The NF system was initially operated in full recirculation mode, with both permeate and retentate recycled to the feed tank for 18 h for studying the effect of different operating conditions such as Mg/Li ratio, applied pressure, and initial feed pH on Li and Mg rejection. The rejection of Li and Mg by both NF90 and NF270 membranes was assessed over a Mg/Li ratio of 5-20. Similarly, different applied pressures (4-10 bar) and feed pH (3-11) were also investigated to achieve optimized operating conditions for Li and Mg rejection. Before the start of each experiment, NF membranes were compacted at 10 bar using ultrapure Milli-Q water for at least 2 h or until the permeate flux stabilized. During all experiments, the permeate samples were collected for analysis at 2, 4, 6, 8 and 18 h.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Membrane distillation system
texts.text: 2.3. Membrane distillation system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The lab-scale direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) systems (Figure S2) comprised a glass feed and distillate tank, membrane cell, a water bath, two recirculation pumps and a heater/chiller. A detailed description of this DCMD setup has been provided elsewhere [39]. Briefly, the DCMD setup was operated by keeping the temperature of the feed and distillate tanks at 40 and 20 °C, respectively. Water from the feed tank (containing NF90 or NF270 permeate) and distillate tank (containing ultrapure Milli-Q water) were continuously passed through the membrane cell at 1 L/min. Due to the temperature difference between both sides, water passed through the polytetrafluoroethylene hydrophobic membrane (Ningbo Changqi Porous Membrane Technology, China) from the warmer side to the cooler side. This reduces the volume of water in the feed tank, and achieves Li enrichment/concentration. The distillate tank was kept on a precision balance (Mettler Toledo Inc., USA) that is connected to a computer for measuring the permeate flux. The DCMD system was operated at 80% water recovery rate for achieving a concentration factor of 5.

texts.orig: 2.4. Analytical methods
texts.text: 2.4. Analytical methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Li and Mg concentration in different samples were analyzed by using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICPOES, 7500CS, Agilent Technologies, Wilmington, DE, USA). Before analysis, each sample was diluted using 5% nitric acid (Suprapur, Sigma-Aldrich, Australia). The linear regression coefficients for all calibration curves were higher than 0.99 for both elements. Prior to each batch of analyses, the ICP-MS was tuned by a multi-element tuning solution. Prior to analysis, the system was calibrated using standard solutions containing 1.56, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 mg/L of each ion. Solution pH and conductivity were measured using an Orion 4-Star Plus pH/conductivity meter (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, USA).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1.1. Effect of Mg/Li ratio
texts.text: 3.1.1. Effect of Mg/Li ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Depending on the membrane type, Li rejection ranged between 63% (by NF90) to 33% (by NF270) at an Mg/Li ratio of 5 (Fig. 1). Although Mg was also detected in the permeate, its rejection by NF270 and NF90 was excellent and varied from 90 to 99%. This is because the Mg ion (hydrated radius 430 pm) is much larger than Li (340 pm), so has a higher rejection. The force of hydration energy can also play an important role in separating divalent and monovalent ions by NF processes. Previously, Pontalier, Ismail [40] reported that higher hydration force is required to extract Mg as compared to Li, and thus, the rejection efficiency of Li was lower than that observed for Mg in this study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NF270 achieved lower rejection for both Mg and Li ions than NF90 (Fig. 1). This is expected given the greater MWCO of the NF270, indicating that the average size of a free volume element is larger. This is consistent with other researchers [22]. By increasing the Mg/Li ratio from 5 to 20, Li rejection increased, particularly for the tighter NF90 membrane (Fig. 1). This is because the total ionic strength increased, which screened the negative charge on the membrane, thus reducing the attractive electrostatic force with the positively charged lithium that helps to drive permeation. By contrast, the Mg rejection reduced slightly (Fig. 1), due to the increased concentration of this ion [20,21]. It can be concluded that any increase in Mg/Li ratio will reduce the purity of the Li in the permeate for both NF270 and NF90 membranes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1.2. Effect of applied pressure
texts.text: 3.1.2. Effect of applied pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 2 illustrates the rejection of Mg and Li as a function of operating pressure. Any change in rejection is generally within the range of experimental error (Fig. 2). However, an increase in applied pressure (up to 6 bar) appears to result in slightly reduced Li rejection for the NF90 membrane. If ions permeate these membranes purely through a solution diffusion mechanism, the ionic flux is expected to be independent of pressure. However, as the water flux increases, this generally leads to a slight increase in rejection, as the permeate concentration is diluted [41]. Small ions such as lithium can also permeate by convective forces, which essentially 'drag' the ion through the membrane with the water. In this case, the ionic flux will increase with pressure. This is the cause of the observed slight reduction in Li rejection with pressure.

texts.text: 3.1.3. Effect of solution pH

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solution pH can influence ion rejection by NF membranes due to the charged nature of the membrane surface that changes with the pH. The effect of pH ranging from 3 to 11 on the rejection of Mg and Li ions was studied by adding NaOH and/or HCl to the feed solution. When the solution pH increased from 3 to 11, Li rejection by NF90 and NF270 membranes fell (Fig. 3). The isoelectric point of the NF270 and NF90 membranes ranges between 3.3-4 [42] and 4.3 [43], respectively. This means that the membranes are positively charged at pH 3. This positively charged membrane repels the positively charged Li ion, leading to strong rejection. As the pH increases, there is an increasing negative charge on the surface of the NF membranes [44,45], which provides an increasing force of attraction with the positively charged Li ions, so that the rejection falls. Similar effects were observed for Mg rejection for the looser NF270. However, the smaller pore size of the NF90 membrane means that charge effects are less important in this case and so the rejection is constant.

texts.orig: 3.2. Performance of an integrated NF-MD for lithium recovery
texts.text: 3.2. Performance of an integrated NF-MD for lithium recovery

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.1. Performance of NF for Li and Mg rejection under optimized conditions
texts.text: 3.2.1. Performance of NF for Li and Mg rejection under optimized conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the performance of NF270 and NF90 membranes presented in section 3.1, the following operating conditions were selected for the NF system to be integrated with the MD process: Mg/Li ratio = 10, applied pressure=8 bar and pH=5. NF-permeate was collected by operating the NF system for a period of 24 h with retentate recirculation, and then used as a feed to the MD system. Under these conditions, Mg rejection by NF90 and NF270 was >99% and 91%, respectively (Fig. 4). On the other hand, Li rejection varied from 66 to 77 and 48-56%, respectively. Notably, Li rejection by both NF membranes increased in the first 8 h, and stabilized at 77% (for NF90) and 56% (for NF270). It can be noted that the Mg/Li molar ratio in the permeate changed from 10 to 0.19 after NF90 treatment, and 10 to 2.1 after NF270 treatment. This shows that lithium penetrated the membranes preferentially and became available for its recovery in the permeate solution.

Relative to pure water, the permeate flux of the DCMD system reduced by 14 and 21% when the permeate of the NF90 and NF270 membranes, respectively, was used as a feed to the DCMD system. The permeate production rate decreased further as the feed concentration increased (Fig. 5). This is because increased salinity of the feed reduced the water activity and reduced the transmembrane vapor pressure difference, which is the driving force of the MD process [47]. Moreover, the increase in feed concentration can also lead to a rise in the viscosity of the feed, thereby increasing the concentration polarisation. The effect of concentration polarization on MD flux following an increase in the concentration of LiCl liquid desiccant has been reported previously [38].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Perspective
texts.text: 3.3. Perspective

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Literature reports suggest that desalination of water by MD can be lead to a significant reduction in water price. The electrical energy consumption of the MD system is expressed as specific electrical energy consumption (SEEC), which is the electrical energy consumed per unit volume of distillate produced (kW h/m 3 ). Compared to the RO process, MD system could consume significantly lower SEEC for seawater desalination ( e.g. , 0.13 compared to approximately 4 kW h/m 3 ) [50]. This comparison demonstrates the advantage of MD over RO for seawater desalination when integrated with solar energy. The operational costs of MD can also be reduced when using low-grade waste heat available on site. Indeed, water production cost as low as $0.26/m 3 has been reported for a seawater MD desalination unit using waste heat [51]. Another study by Drioli et al. [52] compared the economic efficiency of the desalination process using two hybrid systems, namely, NF/RO and NF/RO/MD-crystallization. The thermal energy demand in the latter hybrid system can be offset by improving 100% water recovery, removing disposed brine and recovering valuable salts. For a system consisting of RO and MD crystallization operating at 70% water recovery with a capacity of 739.6 m 3 /h, approximately 3 million dollars of yearly profit can be accrued for selling the sodium chloride salts produced by MD crystallisation [27].

texts.orig: Notably, lithium is mainly recovered from minerals in the mining industry or from salt lake brines. Currently, the average price of lithium is $17/kg. The lithium production costs from mining of spodumene is estimated to be 6-8 $/kg, which is not competitive with respect to the recovery cost from salt lake brines (2-3 $/kg) [1]. Hence, a significant economic benefit can be achieved if lithium along with other salts is recovered from salt lake brine. Overall, this study assumes that there is a trade-off between energy cost and production of salt along with clean water production at high water recoveries. However, a comprehensive techno-economic analysis between the operating cost of the membrane technologies and price for the production of lithium salt needs to carried out.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An integrated membrane process using nanofiltration (NF) and membrane distillation (MD) for the enrichment of lithium (Li) from simulated salt lake brine was investigated in this study. Firstly, two NF membranes of different molecular weight cut-off were studied to extract lithium from the simulated salt lake brine at different operating conditions such as solution chemistry and operating pressure. Li rejection by both the NF90 and NF270 membranes increased after an increase in Mg/Li ratio. On the other hand, Li rejection by both NF membranes decreased with solution pH. The optimum conditions for Li recovery by the NF membranes were an Mg/Li of 10, solution pH of 5 and applied pressure of 8 bar. Under these conditions, the Mg/Li molar ratio changed from 10 to 0.19 after NF90 treatment, and 10 to 2.1 after NF270 treatment. Li rejection by both NF membranes increased in the first 8 h but stabilized at 77% (for NF90) and 56% (for NF270). Li was then further enriched/concentrated by up to 80% by using the MD process. This study demonstrated that an integrated process could recover Li from a salt lake brine with high Mg/Li ratio. However, studies on the influence of other cations, in addition to Mg, coexisting in salt lake brine on lithium separation using NF system need to be investigated. Due to the complex and highly variable composition of the salt lake brine, studies of membrane fouling during long-term operation of real brines are also required.
texts.text: An integrated membrane process using nanofiltration (NF) and membrane distillation (MD) for the enrichment of lithium (Li) from simulated salt lake brine was investigated in this study. Firstly, two NF membranes of different molecular weight cut-off were studied to extract lithium from the simulated salt lake brine at different operating conditions such as solution chemistry and operating pressure. Li rejection by both the NF90 and NF270 membranes increased after an increase in Mg/Li ratio. On the other hand, Li rejection by both NF membranes decreased with solution pH. The optimum conditions for Li recovery by the NF membranes were an Mg/Li of 10, solution pH of 5 and applied pressure of 8 bar. Under these conditions, the Mg/Li molar ratio changed from 10 to 0.19 after NF90 treatment, and 10 to 2.1 after NF270 treatment. Li rejection by both NF membranes increased in the first 8 h but stabilized at 77% (for NF90) and 56% (for NF270). Li was then further enriched/concentrated by up to 80% by using the MD process. This study demonstrated that an integrated process could recover Li from a salt lake brine with high Mg/Li ratio. However, studies on the influence of other cations, in addition to Mg, coexisting in salt lake brine on lithium separation using NF system need to be investigated. Due to the complex and highly variable composition of the salt lake brine, studies of membrane fouling during long-term operation of real brines are also required.
